articles.author: Jon Gambrell, Sally Abou Aljoud, Lisa Mascaro
articles.description: Iran has targeted the world’s busiest international airport and attacked commercial ships. The latest attacks on Wednesday marked an escalation in its campaign aimed at generating global economic pain to pressure the United States and Israel to end its strikes. The conflict has upended trade routes, choked supplies of fuel and fertilizer coming out of the Gulf and threatened air traffic through one of the world’s most-traveled regions. Both sides have dug in, hoping to outlast the other. The United Nation's most powerful body demanded a halt to Iran’s attacks on its Gulf neighbors that threatened the world’s oil supply.
articles.title: Iran targets busiest international airport as UN demands end to Gulf attacks

articles.description: The price of a barrel of Brent crude oil, the international standard, has topped $100 a barrel again just days after it spiked near $120. Oil prices shot more than 9% higher early Thursday as supply concerns worsened with Iranian attacks on commercial shipping around the Strait of Hormuz. U.S. benchmark crude jumped to about $95 a barrel. Markets in Asia fell back as investors absorbed the latest news suggesting the war in the Middle East is far from over. U.S. futures lost more than 1% and the dollar climbed to 159 Japanese yen while the euro fell to $1.1538.
articles.title: Brent crude oil tops $100 a barrel as Iran attacks shipping

articles.author: Matt Sedensky, Wyatte Grantham-Philips
articles.description: A widening war in Iran has halted oil tankers, made targets of refineries and spooked investors worried about the cascading impact of spiking energy prices. In response, the International Energy Agency agreed on Wednesday to release the largest volume of emergency oil reserves in its history, with the Paris-based organization pledging to make 400 million barrels of oil available from its member nations’ stockpiles. The announcement marked a shift in momentum in government response to the war upending the flow of oil, with other global leaders previously indicating reluctance to tap into stockpiles.
articles.title: Oil shock from Iran war prompts countries to open strategic reserves

articles.author: Julia Frankel, Michael Biesecker, Eric Tucker
articles.description: Dozens of Democratic senators are demanding answers from the Trump administration as a growing body of evidence suggests that the U.S. was likely responsible for a strike at an elementary school in Iran that killed over 165 people, many of them children. The letter from more than 45 senators pressed Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth on whether the U.S. was in fact culpable and, if so, what the intended targets were for the strike and what analysis had been done beforehand on the purpose of the building. The senators also raised concerns about the hollowing out of a congressionally mandated office set up specifically to reduce civilian casualties.
articles.title: Senators demand answers on bombing of Iranian school

articles.author: David Bauder
articles.description: The Pentagon has not permitted photographers to cover Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's last two briefings on the war in Iran, and hasn't explained the change in longstanding policy. Relations between the press and the Pentagon have been contentious during the second Trump administration. Most mainstream news reporters are no longer working in the Pentagon building because they would not agree to the Trump administration's new rules for operating there. They have been replaced mostly by news outlets friendly to President Donald Trump. Reporters who gave up desks at the Pentagon have been given day passes to return for Hegseth's briefings, though. The Pentagon would not comment on why photographers have been excluded.
articles.title: Pentagon blocks photographers from Hegseth's briefings

articles.author: Josh Boak
articles.description: The Trump administration is opening a new trade investigation into manufacturing in foreign countries. It’s an effort that comes after the Supreme Court struck down President Donald Trump’s previous use of tariffs. Trump and his team have made clear that they’re seeking to replace the hundreds of billions of dollars in lost revenues by using different laws to establish new tariffs. The administration is starting investigations under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974 to try to charge new import taxes. But U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer told reporters that he didn’t want to prejudge the outcome of the process, even though he said that “the policy remains the same.”
articles.title: Trump administration kicks off new tariff strategy

articles.author: Michael Melia
articles.description: Rural school districts say the Trump administration’s visa changes are jeopardizing a key source of teachers. Many schools hired foreign teachers when they couldn't attract American instructors to remote locations. But visas have become harder to come by under the Trump administration's immigration crackdown. Schools are looking for other ways to fill the gaps. When schools can't hire local replacements for international educators, some are planning to rely more on online instruction. Instructors join a class live and teach via video chat. Elsewhere, school leaders say they may hire uncertified teachers, combine classes or drop course offerings.
articles.title: Trump visa changes squeeze rural schools relying on international teachers

articles.author: Bernard Condon
articles.description: Among dozens of companies competing for Pentagon contracts to supply attack drones, one stands out: Powerus, flush with cash and ballooning in size as it buys up rivals. It also has another advantage. It is partly owned by President Donald Trump’s two oldest sons. The stake is the latest in a string of new investments by the sons, especially Don Jr., in companies with federal contracts. The Trump Organization did not respond to a request to comment on the potential conflict, but issued statement from Eric saying he is proud of his investments.
articles.title: New drone maker partly owned by Trump sons hopes to win Pentagon contracts

articles.author: Farnoush Amiri
articles.description: U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran have damaged at least four cultural and historical sites across Iran. Experts and advocates have raised alarm about the impact of the widening war on protected landmarks that are important to Iranian identity. The speed and extent of the damage have so concerned Iran and Lebanon that they sent a request to the United Nations’ cultural agency, UNESCO, this week to add more sites to its World Heritage enhanced protection list. UNESCO confirmed it's verified damage to a lavish palace in Tehran as well as a 17th century palace and the country's oldest Friday mosque, both in Isfahan.
articles.title: War damages historical sites in Iran

articles.author: Dave Collins
articles.description: A man who was shot by Connecticut police last year and later died had to wait 10 extra minutes for another ambulance to arrive at the scene, after another officer having an anxiety attack took the first one. That's according to a new report released Tuesday by the state Inspector General's Office, which ruled the shooting justified. The report says Dyshan Best was shot last year when he pointed a gun at a Bridgeport police officer. It's not clear whether the delay contributed to Best's death. He died less than two hours after the shooting. Bridgeport police declined to comment on the report and said there will be an internal investigation.
articles.title: Man shot by police in Bridgeport dies after ambulance delay
